coredata.dc:title: A fluorometric and real-time assay for α-glucosidase activity through supramolecular self-assembly and its application for inhibitor screening
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Cong
Qian, Zhaosheng
Qian, Yinjie
Huang, Yuanyuan
Zhao, Meizhi
Ao, Hang
Feng, Hui
coredata.dc:description: A fluorometric biosensor has been developed to sensitively detect α-glucosidase activity and screen α-glucosidase inhibitors based on carbon quantum dots nanoprobe through supramolecular self-assembly. β-Cyclodextrin was covalently bonded to the surface of carbon quantum dots to prepare β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe. The presence of 4-nitrophenol drives self-assembly between β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe and 4-nitrophenol through host-guest recognition. This supramolecular assembly exhibits non-fluorescent nature, which results in strong quenching of the fluorescence of β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe. When 4-nitrophenyl-α-d-glucopyranoside was chosen as the substrate, it would be readily hydrolyzed to 4-nitrophenol in the presence of α-glucosidase. The linear correlation of quenched fluorescence to α-glucosidase level can be used to establish quantitative detection of α-glucosidase activity. The detection limit of this method can be lowered as 0.6U/L, and possesses excellent sensitivity and selectivity. The function of this method to screen inhibitor of α-glucosidase was also assessed using acarbose as the example, and positive results supported its feasibility to screen potential inhibitors of α-glucosidase. This work proposes a new signal transduction mechanism through supramolecular assembly, and provides a sensitive detection method for α-glucosidase activity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Supramolecular assembly
Carbon quantum dots
α-Glucosidase
Fluorometric and real-time assay
Inhibitor screening

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of mono-6-ethylenediamine-β-cyclodextrin-modified carbon quantum dots (β-CD-CQDs) probe
Selectivity test of the α-glucosidase assay
Principle of α-glucosidase detection based on β-cyclodextrin functionalized carbon quantum dots (β-CD-CQDs) nanoprobe
Synthesis and characterization of β-cyclodextrin functionalized carbon quantum dots (β-CD-CQDs) nanoprobe
The detection mechanism for α-Glu activity based on β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe
Real-time fluorescent assay for α-glucosidase activity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of strategy for the detection of α-glucosidase activity and screening of inhibitors based on supramolecular self-assembly between β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe and 4-nitrophenol.
TEM image (A) of β-CD-CQDs and XPS spectrum (B) of β-CD-CQDs. inset: HRTEM image of β-CD-CQDs.
(A) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CQDs and β-CD-CQDs and (B) time-resolved decay curves of CQDs and β-CD-CQDs. Inset: regression equations.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of β-CD-CQDs in the presence of different concentration of 4-nitrophenol. (B) Changes of fluorescence spectra of β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe in the presence of different composition: (a) mere nanoprobe, (b) β-CD-CQDs and NPGlu (200.0μM), (c) β-CD-CQDs, NPGlu (200.0μM) and α-Glu (150.0U/L).

(A)The fluorescence quenching efficiency versus incubation time at different α-Glu levels (50, 100, 200, 300U/L) in the sensing system containing β-CD-CQDs and NPGlu (200.0 μM). (B) Fluorescence spectra of the sensing system containing β-CD-CQDs, NPGlu (200.0μM) and different levels of α-Glu in the range of 0.0–20.0U/L. (C) Calibration curve between fluorescenceintensity of β-CD-CQDs and α-Glu level in the range from 2.2 to 60.0U/L. (D) Selectivity test of the assay toward α-Glu. I0 and I represent the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of different compositions including bovine serum albumin (BSA), tyrosinase (TYR), lysozyme (LYS), glucose oxidase (GOx), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), α-amylase (AMS) and β-galactosidase (β-Gal) and α-Glu (150.0U/L), where the concentration of NPGlu is 200.0μM.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the sensing system containing β-CD-CQDs, NPGlu (200.0μM) and α-Glu (100.0U/L) in the presence of different concentrations of acarbose (from bottom to top): 0.0, 50.0, 100.0, 200.0, 300.0, 400.0 and 500.0μM, respectively. (B) The fitting curve of inhibition efficiency versus concentration of acarbose.

head.ce:title: A fluorometric and real-time assay for α-glucosidase activity through supramolecular self-assembly and its application for inhibitor screening

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University, Jinhua 321004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed to this work equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorometric biosensor has been developed to sensitively detect α-glucosidase activity and screen α-glucosidase inhibitors based on carbon quantum dots nanoprobe through supramolecular self-assembly.

A fluorometric biosensor has been developed to sensitively detect α-glucosidase activity and screen α-glucosidase inhibitors based on carbon quantum dots nanoprobe through supramolecular self-assembly. β-Cyclodextrin was covalently bonded to the surface of carbon quantum dots to prepare β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe. The presence of 4-nitrophenol drives self-assembly between β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe and 4-nitrophenol through host-guest recognition. This supramolecular assembly exhibits non-fluorescent nature, which results in strong quenching of the fluorescence of β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe. When 4-nitrophenyl-α-d-glucopyranoside was chosen as the substrate, it would be readily hydrolyzed to 4-nitrophenol in the presence of α-glucosidase. The linear correlation of quenched fluorescence to α-glucosidase level can be used to establish quantitative detection of α-glucosidase activity. The detection limit of this method can be lowered as 0.6U/L, and possesses excellent sensitivity and selectivity. The function of this method to screen inhibitor of α-glucosidase was also assessed using acarbose as the example, and positive results supported its feasibility to screen potential inhibitors of α-glucosidase. This work proposes a new signal transduction mechanism through supramolecular assembly, and provides a sensitive detection method for α-glucosidase activity.

ce:section.ce:para: α-Glucosidase (α-Glu, EC 3.2.1.20) is a membrane-bound enzyme located at the epithelium of the small intestine, and can efficiently catalyze the cleavage of 1,4-α-glucopyranoside bonds of carbohydrate to release glucose molecules [1]. Therefore, α-glucosidase plays a critical role in controlling postprandial glycemic levels and keeping glucose level within a normal range. The inhibition of α-glucosidase can lead to slowdown of the hydrolysis process of carbohydrates and the absorption of glucose in the small intestine [2], and thus the inhibitors of α-glucosidase function as potential drugs to control the blood glucose level within a normal range and thereby relieve the harm to type II diabetes patients [3,4]. It has been found that α-glucosidase inhibitors could be also used as broad-based anti viral-hepatitis agents [5]. More importantly, α-glucosidase is regarded as a screening biomarker for Pompe disease which is induced by the deficiency of lysosomal acid α-glucosidase [6]. Moreover, diagnosis of azoospermia has potential to be aided by measurement of α-glucosidase activity in seminal plasma because its activity in the seminal plasma was shown to be correlated with functionality of the epididymis [7]. Therefore, it is of great importance to develop sensitive and reliable methods to measure α-glucosidase activity and screen its inhibitors for accurate diagnosis of relevant disease and discovery of potential drugs.

The colorimetric assay is a traditional detection method for α-glucosidase activity and screening inhibitors due to its simplicity and practicability [8]. However, its sensitivity is often limited because the enzyme activity and the effect of inhibitors are quantified by measuring absorbance at 400nm of 4-nitrophenol released from the substrate 4-nitrophenyl-α-d-glucopyranoside. Moreover, the detection is disturbed when the overlap of absorption between inhibitors and 4-nitrophenol takes place. Although much improvement has been made using gold nanoparticles [9–11], the colorimetric assay is still inferior to fluorometric methods in terms of sensitivity and interference. Several fluorogenic substrates including 4-methylumbelliferyl [12], 2-napthyl [13] and resorufin-based glucopyranosides [14] have been synthesized to assay α-glucosidase activity or screen its inhibitors. Recent advance on new alternative to molecular fluorophore has been made. The qualitative detection of α-glucosidase activity was achieved through the electrostatic assembly of alkynylplatinum(II) terpyridyl complexes [15], and the quantification of α-glucosidase was also realized using a fluorescent biosensor based on cationic conjugated polymers [16]. A recent study reported sensitive assay for α-glucosidase based on functionalized CdTe quantum dots, and provided a simple and rapid screening method for α-glucosidase inhibitors [17]. However, most of the preceding assays are not sufficiently sensitive to determine the practical samples by comparing their detection limits to normal value of α-glucosidase in human seminal plasma (29.0U/L in average) [7], while significant improvement of some assays is required in terms of biocompatibility and stability of the probes. Hence it is still in urgent demand for sensitive and novel fluorophore-based assays for α-glucosidase.

Carbon quantum dots (CQD) have begun to play an increasingly important role in biosensor and bioimaging because they possess many merits over conventional dye molecules and semiconductor QDs such as good photostability, low toxicity, good water solubility and excellent biocompatibility [18–21]. Carbon quantum dots have been used to develop biosensors targeting at biological enzymes [22–26] where they show excellent sensing performance. In this study, we design a fluorescent biosensor based on β-cyclodextrin functionalized carbon quantum dots (β-CD-CQDs) and static quenching mechanism to detect α-glucosidase activity and screen its inhibitors. As shown in Scheme 1 , due to hydrophobic character and suitable size of β-cyclodextrin cavity, the β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe can selectively hold 4-nitrophenol which is the hydrolysis product of the substrate 4-nitrophenyl-α-d-glucopyranoside (NPGlu) through the host-guest recognition. When 4-nitrophenol is held by β-cyclodextrin of the nanoprobe, a unique supramolecular assembly forms, and the fluorescence of β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe can be effectively quenched via static quenching mechanism due to the formation of non-fluorescent supramolecular assembly. Based on the fluorescence turn-off of β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe induced by catalytic hydrolysis of the substrate by α-glucosidase, a reliable and real-time assay is developed for the determination of α-glucosidase activity and screening of its inhibitors.

Triple-distilled water was used throughout the experimental process. Activated carbon, mono-6-ethylenediamine-β-cyclodextrin (β-CD), 4-nitrophenyl-α-d-glucopyranoside (NPGlu), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and MgCl2 were purchased from J&K Chemical Co., Ltd. The α-glucosidase (α-Glu, EC 3.2.1.20, 750U) from Saccharomyces cerevisiae, glucose oxidase (GOx, EC 1.1.3.4) from Asper-gilusniger, bovine serum albumin (BSA), tyrosinase (TYR, EC 1.14.18.1) and lysozyme (LYS, EC 3.2.1.17), alkaline phosphatase (ALP, EC 3.1.3.1), α-amylase (AMS) and β-galactosidase (β-Gal, EC 3.2.1.23) were bought from Sigma-Aldrich Company (Shanghai, China). Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4) was prepared by mixing stock solutions of NaCl, KCl, NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4. All reagents were of analytical grade and without any further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of mono-6-ethylenediamine-β-cyclodextrin-modified carbon quantum dots (β-CD-CQDs) probe
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots were first prepared according to our previous papers [25,26], and thus herein the preparation procedure was briefly described as follows. The mixture containing activated carbon (2.0g), sulfuric acid (180mL) and nitric acid (60mL) was heated at 80°C for 5h. Then, the mixture was cooled to room temperature, diluted with triplex-distilled water (2000mL), and further neutralized with sodium hydroxide. A dialysis bag (cut-off of 1000Da) was used to purify carbon quantum dots by the removal of salts and small molecular species. As-purified carbon quantum dots were dried for the following experiments.

The synthesis of mono-6-ethylenediamine-β-cyclodextrin-modified carbon quantum dots probe was modified based on the procedure of aminophenylboronic acid-functionalized carbon quantum dots in our previous report [27]. The CQDs powder (0.05g) was dissolved in deionized water (30mL) and a certain amount of EDC·HCl (0.5g) and NHS (0.5g) were added into the CQDs solution to activate the carboxy groups on the surface of CQDs for 30min. Then a certain amount of mono-6-ethylenediamine-β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) (0.3g) was added to the preceding solution after the pH of the solution was adjusted to 7. The mixed solution was heated at 80°C for 24h. Finally, as-prepared solution was purified with microporous filter (0.22μm) and a dialysis bag (3500Da) to remove excess β-CD, EDC and NHS. After these steps, the β-cyclodextrin-modified carbon quantum dots (β-CD-CQDs) nanoprobe was obtained and used for the following detection of α-glucosidase activity.

For optimization of incubation time for fluorescence quenching by 4-nitrophenol, the fluorescence of β-CD-CQDs solution (10mM PBS, pH 7.4) containing 4-nitrophenol (50.0μM) was recorded after different incubation times of 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 and 7min, respectively. Under the optimum incubation time (2min), the fluorescence quenching of β-CD-CQDs (PBS, pH 7.4) was assessed with continuous addition of 4-nitrophenol, where the concentration of 4-nitrophenol ranged from 0.0 to 90.0μM. After adding a certain amount of 4-nitrophenol every time, 2min of incubation time is required. The fluorescence spectra of resulting solutions with different concentrations of 4-nitrophenol were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 365nm.

The real-time detection of α-glucosidase activity was performed according to the following procedure. The incubation time of α-glucosidase activity assay was first determined with a certain amount of α-glucosidase level. The fluorescence spectra of the sensing system, which contained β-CD-CQDs (PBS, pH 7.4), NPGlu (200.0μM) and Mg2+ (30.0μM), was monitored every 1min after addition of α-glucosidase (200.0U/L). The fluorescence spectra of the sensing system were recorded from 0 to 30min at the excitation wavelength of 365nm. Under the optimal incubation time (25min), the assay for α-glucosidase activity was performed. The specific steps are as follows: First, the standard sensing solution (3.00mL, PBS, pH 7.4) which contained β-CD-CQDs (0.136mg/mL), NPGlu (200.0μM) and Mg2+ (30.0μM) was kept at 37°C for 10min. Second, the fluorescence quenching of the sensing system was assessed with the increase of α-glucosidase, where the level of α-glucosidase ranged from 10.0 to 120.0U/L. Fluorescence spectra of sensing system with different amounts of α-glucosidase were recorded at an optimal excitation wavelength of 365nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity test of the α-glucosidase assay
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the selectivity of this assay toward α-glucosidase, seven different biological species including bovine serum albumin (BSA), tyrosinase (TYR), lysozyme (LYS), glucose oxidase (GOx), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), α-amylase (AMS) and β-galactosidase (β-Gal) were selected. First, the fluorescence spectra of the sensing system containing β-CD-CQDs (PBS, pH 7.4), NPGlu (200.0μM) and Mg2+ (30.0μM) was regarded as the control. Then, the fluorescence spectra of the every sensing system, which were incubated at 37°C for 30min, were monitored in the presence of different enzymes. The level of each enzyme was maintained at 200.0U/L.

Acarbose, which is a commercial α-glucosidase inhibitor and diabetes drug in clinical treatment, was selected as a model inhibitor to evaluate the screening feasibility of our method. The inhibitor of α-glucosidase screening experiment was performed according to the following procedure. The assay solutions (3.0mL, PBS, pH 7.4) containing β-CD-CQDs, NPGlu (200.0μM), Mg2+ (30.0μM), α-glucosidase (200.0U/L) and different concentrations of acarbose ranging from 0.0 to 500.0μM were prepared. The fluorescence of the resulting mixtures was recorded, respectively, after 20min of incubation time at 37°C.

The morphologies of all samples were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), which was performed on a JEOL Model 2100F instrument with an accelerating voltage of 200kV. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analyses were conducted using a Kratos Axis ULTRA X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The UV–vis spectra were recorded on a Perkin-Elmer Lambda 950 spectrometer. The photoluminescence spectra were conducted on a Perkin-Elmer Model LS 55 system, and lifetimes were determined using an Edinburgh Instruments Model FLS980 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of α-glucosidase detection based on β-cyclodextrin functionalized carbon quantum dots (β-CD-CQDs) nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which are prepared through chemical oxidation of activated carbon powder, are selected as the fluorophore because CQDs possess many excellent properties such as excellent aqueous solubility, stable fluorescence emission, excellent biocompatibility and abundant surface groups like carboxyl group. β-Cyclodextrin (β-CD) as a well-known host molecule, which consists of seven glucose units linked one to another by 1–4 glycoside bonds, is capable of holding some hydrophobic molecules inside the cavities such as 4-nitrophenol. In our previous work [27], β-CD-modified carbon quantum dots were prepared through unstable boronic ester bonds between boronic acid-functionalized carbon quantum dots and hydropropyl-β-cyclodextrin, but the obtained nanoprobe was found to be unstable under acidic and neutral conditions due to instability of boronic ester moiety, which makes this nanoprobe unsuitable to detect targets of interest in neutral and acidic solutions. To overcome this shortcoming, the mono-6-ethylenediamine-β-cyclodextrin (eda-β-CD) is selected as the receptor of 4-nitrophenol to modify carbon quantum dots (CQDs) because its ethylenediamine groups are able to form amide bond with carboxyl group of CQDs. Scheme 1 shows schematic illustration of assay strategy for α-glucosidase activity based on supramolecular assembly between β-CD-CQDs and 4-nitrophenol. It is well known that the most common substrate towards the detection of α-glucosidase activity is 4-nitrophenyl-α-d-glucopyranoside (NPGlu). Under the catalysis of α-glucosidase, hydrolysis reaction of NPGlu can generate 4-nitrophenol which can easily enter the hydrophobic cavity of β-CD and form a stable inclusion complex due to the hydrophobicity of 4-nitrophenol and right size match between 4-nitrophenol and the cavity of β-CD. The formation of inclusion complex leads to the disappearance of the fluorescence signal. Because 4-nitrophenol effectively quenches the fluorescence of β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe, we design a facile fluorimetric assay for α-glucosidase activity using 4-nitrophenyl-α-d-glucopyranoside (NPGlu) as the substrate. The design route is as follows: α-glucosidase could catalyze the hydrolysis reaction of NPGlu and the hydrolysis product, 4-nitrophenol, is able to quench the fluorescence of the nanoprobe by static quenching mechanism due to the formation of a supramolecular assembly. The fluorescence quenching level is correlated to the amount of 4-nitrophenol from the hydrolysis of NPGlu. Therefore, this strategy can be applied to design accurately quantitative detection method for real-time evaluating α-glucosidase activity and screening its inhibitors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of β-cyclodextrin functionalized carbon quantum dots (β-CD-CQDs) nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots which are prepared by chemical oxidation of activated carbon according to our previous papers [23,24] are selected as the fluorophore of the nanoprobe. The as-prepared CQDs possess strong yellowish green fluorescence, highly aqueous solubility and rich carboxylic acid groups. The mono-6-ethylenediamine-β-cyclodextrin (eda-β-CD) which contains an amino group is used to modify CQDs. β-Cyclodextrin functionalized carbon quantum dots nanoprobe is synthesized with CQDs and eda-β-CD through a condensation reaction. As-prepared β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe is characterized by TEM, XPS, UV-vis, and fluorescence spectroscopy, respectively. TEM image in Fig. 1 A shows that size distribution of β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe falls in the range from 2 to 8nm which is in consistence with that of mere CQDs, and clearly crystalline structure can be noticed in HR-TEM image. The XPS spectrum in Fig. 1B illustrates that the β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe is composed of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen. The binding energy signals at 284.4eV, 532.0eV and 339.3eV correspond to C (CC), O (CONH, OH) and N (CONH) in the nanoprobe, respectively. Compared with that of pure CQDs (Fig. S1), the signal from N atoms indicates that mono-6-ethylenediamine-β-cyclodextrin (eda-β-CD) has been covalently linked on the surface of CQDs through the amide moiety. It can be seen in Fig. S2 that the obvious absorption peaks can’t be observed for CQDs whereas β-CD-CQDs exhibits an apparent absorption peaks at 260nm. This distinction in UV–vis spectra between CQDs and β-CD-CQDs also provides another proof for the preparation of β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe. Fig. 2 A shows excitation and emission fluorescence spectra of β-CD-CQD nanoprobe and pure CQDs. β-CD-CQDs probe in aqueous solution displays bright green fluorescence under the UV lamp and an emission peak at 450nm; however, pure CQDs in aqueous solution exhibit bright yellow fluorescence and an emission peak at 530nm. Comparison of emission peak between β-CD-CQDs probe and pure CQDs also demonstrates that β-CD-CQDs probe has been successfully synthesized. This significant blue-shift in fluorescence of β-CD-CQDs is attributed to the covalent binding of amino group of β-CD to the surface of CQDs because it is generally observed that the introduction of nitrogen atom or amino group can lead to the blue shift of fluorescence peak for functionalized CQDs [28,29]. As illustrated in Fig. 2B, time-resolved fluorescence decay curves demonstrate different fluorescence lifetimes between β-CD-CQDs probe and pure CQDs, where the lifetime of β-CD-CQDs probe is estimated to be 3.98ns, much longer than that of CQDs (2.31ns). According to the relative method, the fluorescence quantum yield of β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe is calculated to be 3.0% using quinine sulfate as the standard reference. FTIR of CQDs and β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe are showed in Fig. S3. It can be seen that the broad peak at 3000cm−1 is assigned to secondary amine (NH), and the absorption peak at about 1500cm−1 is assigned to carbonyl group (CO) for β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe, which confirms that β-CD has been bonded to the surface of carbon quantum dots. Moreover, stability test of the nanoprobe in Fig. S4 shows that the nanoprobe is stable under irradiation of natural light within 30days and its fluorescence intensity only decreases by 20% under irradiation of UV light for 8h. The experimental results prove that the nanoprobe can be stored in natural light for months with almost no apparent change in fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection mechanism for α-Glu activity based on β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: It has been reported that 4-nitropheol, which acts as an electron acceptor, can effectively quench the fluorescence of CdTe quantum dots through intramolecular photoinduced electron transfer process [30]. The fact in Fig. S5 that the fluorescence of CQDs continuously decreases along with addition of 4-nitrophenol concentration in the range of 0.0–52.0mM indicates that 4-nitrophenol is able to quench the fluorescence of mere CQDs. This phenomenon gives us a revelation that it is possible that PET process takes place between CQDs and 4-nitrophenol. Eda-β-CD is used to modify CQDs because a supramolecular self-assembly process between β-CD-CQDs and 4-nitrophenol can occur to form an inclusion complex through the host-guest recognition. Thus, we evaluate the feasibility for fluorescence quenching of the nanoprobe by 4-nitrophenol. From Fig. 3 A, one can observe that fluorescence intensity of β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe gradually decreases as the concentration of 4-nitropheol is stepwisely increased in the range of 0.0–90.0μM. The small amount of 4-nitropheol (90.0μM) causes the quenching efficiency of 75% whereas a very large amount of 4-nitropheol (52.0mM) is required to attain the same quenching efficiency for CQDs. The large difference of quenching efficiency between β-CD-CQDs probe and CQDs indicates that the self-assembly between β-CD-CQDs probe and 4-nitropheol plays an important role in fluorescence signal transduction. Because it has reported that β-CD and 4-nitrophenol can form a stable inclusion complex through the host-guest recognition [31] and this significant interaction was also revealed for modified CdTe quantum dots [30], it is speculated that the formation of the non-fluorescent inclusion complex is responsible for the strong fluorescence quenching. According to Stern-Volmer equation, the quenching mechanisms for β-CD-CQDs probe and CQDs by 4-nitrophenol were further explored. The linear fitting curves between fluorescence intensity and the amount of 4-nitrophenol were obtained as shown in Figs. S6 and S7 respectively. According to their regression equations and Stern-Volmer equation I0/I=1+Ks[Q]=1+kqτ0[Q], the values for kq for β-CD-CQDs probe and CQDs are 1.1×1013 Lmol−1 s−1 and 8.7×109 Lmol−1 s−1 respectively. In comparison with the value (109–1010 Lmol−1 s−1) for collision-controlled quenching, the fluorescence quenching for CQDs belongs to dynamic quenching which may be due to a PET pathway whereas the quenching for the nanoprobe can be classified as static quenching. The confirmation of these quenching mechanisms was also supported by the change in lifetime as a function of quencher concentration as shown in Fig. S7. The values τ0/τ for CQDs are linearly correlated to the concentration of 4-nitrophenol, further supporting the dynamic quenching. The values τ0/τ for β-CD-CQDs probe are almost identical as a function of the amount of 4-nitrophenol, indicating that the formation of non-fluorescent inclusion complex between β-CD-CQDs probe and 4-nitrophenol is responsible for this severe fluorescence quenching.

The feasibility of this method for α-glucosidase assay was assessed using NPGlu as the substrate as displayed in Fig. 3B. The addition of a certain amount of NPGlu (200.0μM) only has a negligible effect on the fluorescence of β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe; however, the fluorescence of β-CD-CQDs nanoprobe is strongly quenched after adding 150.0U/L of α-Glu within 25min of incubation time, where a quenching efficiency of 75% was attained. Therefore, it is feasible to develop a fluorometric assay for α-Glu activity based on this specific supramoleclar recognition and static quenching mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real-time fluorescent assay for α-glucosidase activity
ce:section.ce:para: The optimization of incubation time for hydrolysis of NPGlu with different levels of α-Glu (50.0, 100.0, 150.0 and 200.0U/L) was first conducted. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the quenching levels of sensing system progressively increase along with the rise of incubation time from 0.0 to 30.0min. For the four α-Glu levels, the quenching levels have little change over 25min of incubation time. During enzymatic hydrolysis reaction, the higher level of α-Glu certainly leads to a more rapid hydrolysis of substrate and causes a more rapid increase in quenching efficiency. Under optimum condition, the fluorescence of the sensing system containing β-CD-CQDs, NPGlu (200.0μM) and Mg2+ (30μM) is monitored after the addition of different levels of α-Glu. Fig. 4B shows the declining trend in fluorescence intensity of sensing system as the increase of α-Glu level in the range from 0.0 to 120.0U/L. When the level of α-Glu is 100.0U/L, the fluorescence of sensing system arrives minimum and keeps stable. The relationship between the fluorescence intensity and α-Glu activity is obtained showed in Fig. 4C. The regression equation can be expressed as y=−9.72x+999.5 with R2 =0.990. The detection limit based on three times standard deviations is estimated to be 0.66U/L, and the linear scope is from 2.2 to 60.0U/L. Moreover, since it is speculated that the amount of NPGlu may affect analytical performance of this method, another two concentrations of NPGlu including 150.0μM and 300.0μM were chosen to evaluate this assay for α-Glu activity. As shown in Figs. S8 and S9, similar relations between fluorescence intensity and α-Glu activity were obtained. According to their regression equations, the detection limits for the assays using 150.0μM and 300.0μM of substrate are 0.72U/L and 0.64U/L, respectively. One can note that the amount of NPGlu in the assay would not significantly impact the analytical performance when its concentration is over 150.0μM. Overall, the detection limit of our method is lower than those for colorimetric assays based on gold nanoclusters (4U/L or 1U/L) [9,11], that based on β-cyclodextrin coated CdTe quantum dots (3.7U/L) and that based on electrochemical assay (40U/L)[32], and possesses sufficient sensitivity for practical sample such as human seminal plasma (29.0U/L in average) [7]. To further confirm the superiority of our method in sensitivity, we performed colorimetric analysis of α-Glu by determining the absorbance of 4-nitrophenol hydrolyzed from the substrate NPGlu in the presence of α-Glu. Although this colorimetric detection for α-Glu has been reported, detailed data in analytical performance including detection limit was not shown in the paper [8]. Quantitative detection of 4-nitrophenol by quantitating the absorbance at 410nm was first established as shown in Fig. S10A. The calibration curve in Fig. S10B shows that the absorbance is linearly related to the amount of 4-nitropheol in the range of 0.0–26.0μM. The absorbance at 410nm from 4-nitrophenol increases as the level of α-Glu is elevated from 0.0 to 150U/L, and the corresponding calibration curve in Fig. S10D can be expressed in the following equation: y=0.00528x−0.0745 where R2 is 0.998. From the calibration curve, the detection limit is determined to be 4.1U/L based on three times standard deviations. One can note that the detection limits of our method varying from 0.64 to 0.72U/L are much lower than that based on this conventional colorimetric method, which confirms the advantage of our method over the traditional assay in terms of sensitivity for detection of α-glucosidase activity. Matrix effect on our assay was further quantitatively evaluated by standard addition method using 50-fold dilution of calf serum as the complex matrix. Table S1 displays that the recovery ratio of measured value to added value for six samples containing different levels of α-Glu in the range of 10.0–60.0U/L varies from 96.51% to 98.25%, indicating that this assay is capable to accurately quantify α-Glu level in such a complex matrix as real samples.

In addition, selectivity test of this fluorometric assay was also conducted to assess the possible interference. Under same conditions, seven different biological species including bovine serum albumin (BSA), tyrosinase (TYR), lysozyme (LZM), glucose oxidase (GOx), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), α-amylase(AMS) and β-galactosidase (β-Gal) are separately added into assay solution containing β-CD-CQDs, NPGlu (200.0μM) and Mg2+ (30.0μM), and then the fluorescence is monitored. It can be seen from Fig. 4D that the presence of BSA, TYR, LZM, GOx, ALP, AMS and β-Gal hardly affects the fluorescence intensity of sensing system, however, the existence of α-Glu (150.0U/L) causes a dramatic decline of fluorescence intensity with the quenching efficiency of 68.8%. Therefore, the result demonstrates that our method based on β-cyclodextrin-modified carbon quantum dots possesses high selectivity towards α-Glu.

It is well known that it is of importance to develop convenient means to screen new and more effective α-Glu inhibitors for treatment of diabetes mellitus. In order to test and confirm that our method is able to be employed to screen potential inhibitors of α-Glu, acarbose, as a well-known drug for diabetes mellitus [3,4], is taken as the example to evaluate the screening function of this assay. As shown in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence intensity of the sensing system containing β-CD-CQDs, Mg2+ (30.0μM) and NP-Glu (200.0μM) is quenched by 61% after the addition of α-Glu (100.0U/L) in the absence of acarbose. However, with increasing acarbose concentration from 50.0 to 500.0μM, fluorescence quenching level of sensing system decreases from 57.4% to 8.8%. The inhibition efficiency was found to be linearly related to acarbose concentration as shown in Fig. 5B. The half-maximal inhibitory efficiency of inhibitor (IC50) value can be used to assess inhibitory efficiency of the inhibitor. Based on the linear plot of inhibition efficiency versus concentration of acarbose, the IC50 of acarbose is determined to be 319μM, where the calibration equation can be expressed as y=−3.98+0.169x with R2 =0.99. Compared with the value for IC50 based on cationic conjugated polymer (IC50 =0.60mM) [16], our method is more sensitive to assess the inhibition of the potential inhibitors. Therefore, it is supportive that this method is capable of screening inhibitors from potential alternatives.

In summary, β-cyclodextrin-modified carbon quantum dots (β-CD-CQDs) nanoprobe was prepared and utilized to design a convenient and efficient real-time fluorometric method for detection of α-glucosidase activity and screening of its inhibitors. In this method, a supramolecular recognition event between β-cyclodextrin and 4-nitrophenol and the non-fluorescent character of the formed inclusion complex were utilized to achieve specific fluorescence switching off process. After the substrate NPGlu was hydrolyzed to 4-nitrophenol in the presence of α-glucosidase, the fluorescence would be quenched. The quenched fluorescence can be related to the level of α-glucosidase, and thus this relation can be used to measure the activity of α-glucosidase. As-established assay possesses excellent detection limit in comparison to the previous methods, and is sufficiently sensitive for practical sample. The assessment of screening inhibitor using acarbose as an example shows this assay is able to effectively screen inhibitors of α-glucosidase. This work provides a sensitive fluorometric assay for α-glucosidase, and also broadens the application of carbon quantum dots in bioanalysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21675143, 21405142 and 21275131) and Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (Grant No. LY17B050003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.01.150.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

A prospect for pyrrolidine iminosugars as antidiabetic α-glucosidase inhibitors
ɑ-Glucosidase inhibitors as potential broad based anti-viral agents
Determination of acid ɑ-glucosidase protein: evaluation as a screening marker for Pompe disease and other lysosomal storage disorders
Seminal plasma α-glucosidase activity and male infertility
High-throughput microtiter plate-based chromogenic assays for glycosidase inhibitors
Sensitive colorimetric assays for α-glucosidase activity and inhibitor screening based on unmodified gold nanoparticles
Gold nanoparticles for biology and medicine

A colorimetric method for α-glucosidase activity assay and its inhibitor screening based on aggregation of gold nanoparticles induced by specific recognition between phenylenediboronic acid and 4-aminophenyl-α-d-glucopyranoside
Measurement of ɑ-glucosidase activity in serum from patients with cystic fibrosisor pancreatitis
α-Glucosidase as a specific epididymal enzyme marker its validity for the etiologic diagnosis of azoospermia
A new resorufin-based α-glucosidase assay for high-throughput screening

Novel fluorescent biosensor for α-glucosidase inhibitor screening based on cationic conjugated polymers
Sensitive fluorimetric assays for α-glucosidase activity and inhibitor screening based on β-cyclodextrin-coated quantum dots
Semiconductor quantum dots for photodynamic therapy
Graphene quantum dots: emergent nanolights for bioimaging, sensors, catalysis and photovoltaic devices
Glowing graphene quantum dots and carbon dots: properties, syntheses and biological applications
Functionalized carbon quantum dots with dopamine for tyrosinase activity monitoring and inhibitor screening: in vitro and intracellular investigation
A real-time fluorescent assay for the detection of alkaline phosphatase activity based on carbon quantum dots
Carbon quantum dots-based recyclable real-time fluorescence assay for alkaline phosphatase with adenosine triphosphate as substrate
A fluorometric assay for acetylcholinesterase activity and inhibitor screening with carbon quantum dots
Reversible fluorescent nanoswitch based on carbon quantum dots nanoassembly for real-time acid phosphatase activity monitoring
A fluorometric assay for alkaline phosphatase activity based on β-cyclodextrin-modified carbon quantum dots through host-guest recognition
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots with oxygen-rich functional groups
Surface functionalization of graphene quantum dots with small organic molecules from photoluminescence modulation to bioimaging applications: an experimental and theoretical investigation
β-Cyclodextrin-modified CdSe/ZnS quantum dots for sensing and chiroselective analysis
Measurement of binding constant of cyclodextrin by ultrafiltration
Electrochemical assay of α-glucosidase activity and the inhibitor screening in cell medium

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Cong Tang studies at Zhejiang Normal University, and obtained his BSc in chemistry in 2013. His research focus on the fluorometric assays for enzyme activity based on carbon quantum dots.

Zhaosheng Qian majored in chemistry in Nanjing University, China, and received his PhD in 2009. He started his academic career at Zhejiang Normal University, and promoted as Associate Professor in 2011. He engages in novel luminescent chemo-/biosensors with molecular probe and a variety of nanomaterials, and has published more than 50 scientific papers.
Yinjie Qian is an undergraduate at Zhejiang Normal University, and is working on a project of quantifying glucosidase activity based on carbon quantum dots under supervision of Dr. Hui Feng.
Yuanyuan Huang is studying at Zhejiang Normal University for her Master degree in chemistry. She is working on synthesis of luminescent copper nanoclusters and its application in bioanalysis.
Meizhi Zhao is a graduate of Dr. Feng’s research group, and she is mainly engaged on novel reversible nanoswitches and their use in assaying enzyme activity.
Hang Ao completed her bachelor degree in chemistry at Zhejiang Normal University. She contributes to design and construction of reversible luminescence nanoswitch based on dynamic covalent chemistry.

Hui Feng studied chemistry in Nanjing University, and completed her PhD in 2009. She works at Zhejiang Normal University as an associate professor. Her research interests focus on the development of novel biosensors targeting at DNA, proteins and peptides.
